message.content:
Great. With the collections identified (movies and users), here are practical next steps to explore and work with your data. I’ve included example commands you can run in your MongoDB client (mongosh or a driver).

1) Peek at sample documents
- Movies: db.movies.find({}).limit(5).pretty()
- Users: db.users.find({}).limit(5).pretty()

2) Check data shape and counts
- Rough counts: db.movies.estimatedDocumentCount(), db.users.estimatedDocumentCount()
- Look at a sample document to see fields: db.movies.findOne(), db.users.findOne()

3) Review existing indexes
- Movies: db.movies.getIndexes()
- Users: db.users.getIndexes()

4) Add helpful indexes (if not present)
- On common query fields, e.g.:
  - db.movies.createIndex({ title: 1 })
  - db.users.createIndex({ email: 1 }, { unique: true })

5) Quick data profiling with aggregation
- Group by a field (e.g., genre) to summarize:
  - db.movies.aggregate([{ $group: { _id: "$genre", count: { $sum: 1 } } }])
- Count active users, etc., based on a status field if you have one.

6) Check for missing or inconsistent data
- Find documents missing a field:
  - db.movies.find({ title: { $exists: false } }).limit(10)
  - db.users.find({ email: { $exists: false } }).limit(10)

7) Basic data quality and validation
- If you use schemas (via schemas in MongoDB or your app layer), review required fields, types, and constraints.
- Consider setting up simple validators if you’re on a modern MongoDB version.

8) Security and maintenance
- Review user roles and authentication settings.
- Plan backups and, if needed, replication/sharding strategy for scale.

If you tell me your goal (e.g., “find users who signed up last 30 days,” “list movies by genre,” “ensure emails are unique,” or “set up an index for fast lookups”), I can tailor exact commands and a small, ready-to-run plan for you. Would you like me to draft a specific query or index plan based on a particular use case?
